common_name: Encore Autumn Chiffon Azalea (Reblooming Light-Pink Azalea)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade with at least 4 hours of direct sun; Autumn Chiffon is one of the most compact Encore varieties and is excellent for smaller planting spaces and container use.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade with at least 4 hours of direct sun; Encore Autumn Chiffon is a refined, compact reblooming azalea producing medium-sized, single, very light pink to blush-pink flowers in spring and again in summer and fall — the palest and most delicate flower color in the common Encore azalea lineup, with petals so light they appear nearly white in bright sun. The softly colored flowers create a particularly elegant effect in combination with deep green foliage and make Autumn Chiffon one of the most useful Encore selections for sophisticated color schemes where pastel blends are desired. The compact habit stays 2–3 feet tall and wide, fitting neatly into smaller foundation plantings, mixed shrub borders, and large containers. Like all Encore azaleas, it requires more direct sun than traditional azaleas for reliable fall rebloom — a minimum of 4 hours and ideally 5–6 hours of daily direct sun produces the best fall bloom quality. The semi-evergreen foliage holds through zone 9 winters with minimal damage. Fertilize with an azalea-specific acidic fertilizer immediately after the spring bloom to support subsequent reblooming.

common_name: Encore Autumn Sangria Azalea (Reblooming Bicolor Azalea)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade with 4–6 hours of direct sun; Autumn Sangria is a vigorous-growing Encore variety that may need occasional pruning immediately after the spring bloom to maintain a tidy size.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade with 4–6 hours of direct sun; Encore Autumn Sangria is a distinctive reblooming azalea with flowers in a beautiful, wine-rose to deep rosy-pink color with a lighter center — a rich, saturated color with the warmth of burgundy and the brightness of pink combined in a tone reminiscent of the wine it is named for. This vibrant color is more intense and warm-toned than the cooler lilac-purple of Autumn Royalty or the clear red of Autumn Fire, making it a good companion for rose, salmon, and warm-toned plantings. Autumn Sangria is a medium-large Encore selection, growing 3–4 feet tall and wide with vigorous habit. The spring bloom is particularly heavy, followed by reliable summer and fall rebloom flushes when given adequate sun. In zone 9, Autumn Sangria typically begins its spring bloom in mid-March and continues in flushes through November. The semi-evergreen foliage is attractive and holds well through zone 9 winters. Apply a layer of pine bark mulch 2–3 inches deep to protect the shallow root system from temperature extremes and moisture loss during summer heat.

common_name: Firepower Nandina (Firepower Heavenly Bamboo, Dwarf Nandina)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun to full shade; Firepower is one of the most adaptable landscape shrubs available — tolerating virtually any soil and light condition — but develops its most vivid red winter color in full sun with some cold.

directions: Plant in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun to full shade; Firepower is the most widely planted dwarf nandina cultivar — a compact, non-suckering selection reaching 18–30 inches tall and wide with outstanding year-round foliage interest and exceptional winter color. In summer, the fine-textured, compound leaves are chartreuse-green to lime-green; in fall and winter they transition through orange to brilliant scarlet-red to crimson — the most vivid winter color of any nandina in cultivation. Firepower is sterile (producing no berries), which eliminates the wildlife spread concerns associated with the species nandina, whose berries are spread by birds and can cause nandina to naturalize in natural areas. The dwarf, non-invasive growth habit and brilliant winter color have made Firepower one of the most-planted foundation shrubs across zone 6–11 over the past two decades. It is exceptional for year-round container culture, low border plantings, and mass ground cover in conditions from full sun to full shade — though the best red winter color develops in sun exposure. Extremely drought-tolerant once established. No serious pests or diseases affect established plants. No pruning is required; if plants become too dense over many years, cut to near ground level in late winter to rejuvenate.

common_name: Gulf Stream Nandina (Gulf Stream Heavenly Bamboo)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Gulf Stream develops the most vivid blue-green summer color and richest winter-red color with 4–6 hours of direct sun — in deep shade the summer foliage color is more muted green.

directions: Plant in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Gulf Stream is an outstanding mid-sized compact nandina cultivar that fills the gap between the very dwarf Firepower and the full-size species — reaching a tidy 30–42 inches with a dense, rounded, upright-mounding habit and exceptional seasonal foliage interest. The distinctive characteristic of Gulf Stream is its brilliant blue-green summer foliage — more intensely blue-colored than most nandinas — followed by outstanding orange to red to burgundy winter coloring. It is a semi-sterile selection that produces few berries, reducing the invasive spread concerns associated with the species. Gulf Stream was selected by the late nurseryman John Hampton Brinkley of Lewisville, North Carolina, and has been widely promoted by the National Arboretum as an outstanding landscape selection. The medium size, dense habit, and excellent foliage color make Gulf Stream appropriate for foundation plantings, mixed shrub borders, and mass plantings where neither the very dwarf Firepower nor the large standard species is appropriate. Like all nandinas, it is adaptable to a wide range of soils and light conditions, extremely drought-tolerant once established, and resistant to deer browsing.

common_name: Harbour Dwarf Nandina (Dwarf Heavenly Bamboo, Creeping Nandina)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Harbour Dwarf spreads slowly by underground runners to form a colony — allow sufficient space at planting for this spreading habit, or use as an intentional ground cover.

directions: Plant in moist to dry, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Harbour Dwarf is a low, spreading nandina cultivar that grows differently from most selections — rather than forming an upright clump, it spreads slowly by underground runners to form a loose, arching, low-mounding colony of fine-textured, airy foliage reaching 18–24 inches tall with an indefinitely spreading habit. This spreading, groundcover quality makes Harbour Dwarf useful for softening slopes, covering areas under trees, and naturalizing in larger landscape areas where a low, informal groundcover effect is desired. The very fine-textured, compound foliage is more delicate-looking than other nandina cultivars, with a graceful, somewhat weeping quality. The foliage shows good red coloration in winter, particularly in full sun. While most compact nandina cultivars are essentially sterile, Harbour Dwarf may produce scattered berries — the bird-spread invasive potential should be considered before planting near natural areas. The spreading habit and delicate foliage texture make it one of the most attractive nandinas for informal and naturalistic landscape styles. Its height and spreading nature make it excellent as a transition plant between shrub borders and lawn areas.
